findings.finding: The plan's core happy-path dispatch — 'dispatch to the gate-aware calibrate path that child 4 adds to calibrate_criterion' — depends on an unverified capability surface. Today, calibrate_criterion is exclusively plan-review-aware (eval_solver._criterion_id only consults plan_review.registry.by_id; criterion_prompt_id maps every id to plan-review-<id>). Child 4's ticket (rebar:8153-e9b5-5706-49c8) is unfetchable (404), so the concrete API change it will introduce — whether a new `gate=` kwarg, a second function, or a branch inside calibrate_criterion — is unspecified. The CLI dispatch code this ticket writes must know the exact call-site shape to pass a code-review criterion id correctly; without that, the integration cannot be verified as feasible and the dispatch code risks silently routing a code-review id through the plan-review solver (which will produce 'unknown criterion').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (inline comment, AGENTS.md, or design note) the extension point for adding a third gate to the admission check, OR extract the multi-registry membership check into a shared utility (e.g., a `registry.resolve_gate(criterion_id, repo_root)` helper) so the CLI preflight delegates rather than enumerates gates.
evidence: 'Admit iff present in exactly one gate; dispatch to the gate-aware calibrate path'
'If the id is active in both gates, reject as ambiguous'
No mention of extracting the multi-gate membership check into a shared registry utility or documenting where future gates would register
findings.finding: The one-gate resolution rule ('admit iff present in exactly one gate; reject as ambiguous if in both') is a CLI-layer business rule hardcoded in the preflight. If a third gate is added in a future ticket, a developer must remember to update this admission logic in the CLI rather than updating a single shared resolver. The plan's rejected-alternative note documents the UX tradeoff but not the extension point.
scenarios: A third gate (e.g., 'integration_review') is added; the CLI preflight still only checks two registries and silently ignores the third, giving a wrong 'unknown' error for valid ids in the new gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level helper such as `resolve_criterion_gate(criterion_id, repo_root) -> Literal['plan_review','code_review'] | None | 'ambiguous'` in a shared location (e.g., `src/rebar/llm/registry_util.py`). The CLI preflight calls this helper and handles the three outcomes. New gates register in this helper only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the Approach section explaining *why* gate resolution belongs in the CLI preflight layer (e.g., 'early rejection with a user-actionable message before touching fixtures, consistent with the existing preflight pattern') rather than inside `calibrate_criterion`.
evidence: The Approach section describes the mechanism steps but the positive case reads only as 'the one-gate rule covers the common path and only genuine cross-gate ambiguity needs operator input' — this explains scope coverage, not why the preflight is the right layer vs. pushing gate detection into calibrate_criterion.
The rejected alternative ('Adding a criteria eval --gate flag — heavier UX for a rare case') is framed as a cost argument against an alternative, not as a positive rationale for the chosen boundary.
findings.finding: The plan's Approach section describes *what* the mechanism does (dual-registry lookup, one-gate dispatch, ambiguity rejection) but does not state a positive rationale for WHY this is the right seam — i.e., why the gate resolution belongs in the CLI preflight layer rather than inside `calibrate_criterion` itself. The only rationale given is the negation of the rejected `--gate` flag alternative, not an affirmative case for the chosen design boundary.
scenarios: An implementer may choose the wrong abstraction layer — e.g., moving all gate logic into calibrate_criterion — because the plan does not explain why early CLI-level rejection is the right design choice here (user-actionable message before touching fixtures, consistent with existing preflight pattern at lines 143-155).
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the Approach section: 'Resolving the gate at the CLI admission boundary (rather than inside `calibrate_criterion`) preserves the existing preflight contract — a clear, early, user-actionable error before any fixture I/O — and keeps `calibrate_criterion` a gate-agnostic calibration engine.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_criteria_eval.py` to the scope section (or explicitly acknowledge it as a pre-existing consumer that was verified not to require changes) so reviewers know the existing CLI tests are accounted for.
evidence: Plan Scope section names only `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py` and `tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py`.

`tests/unit/test_criteria_eval.py` lines 119-134 contain `test_cli_unknown_criterion_errors`, `test_cli_missing_id_errors`, and `test_cli_absent_fixture_errors` — all invoke `_criteria(["eval", ...])` and implicitly depend on the plan-review-only preflight behavior (e.g. `test_cli_unknown_criterion_errors` passes a non-plan-review id and expects exit 1).
The dual-registry lookup changes the semantics of `_criteria_eval`'s preflight: a code-review id that was formerly 'unknown' might now admit or ambiguate, altering what the existing tests assert.
findings.finding: The existing `tests/unit/test_criteria_eval.py` contains CLI tests (`test_cli_unknown_criterion_errors`, `test_cli_absent_fixture_errors`, `test_cli_missing_id_errors`) that exercise the exact preflight block in `_criteria_eval` (lines 143-155) being rewritten, but this file is not enumerated as a scope target or acknowledged as a consumer that may need updating.
scenarios: After the plan is implemented, `test_cli_unknown_criterion_errors('NOPE-not-a-criterion')` passes both registries and still exits 1 — likely fine — but no review confirmed this.
If a new ambiguous-id test is added to the shared file without verifying the existing CLI tests still pass, a subtle regression could ship silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Scope section: '`tests/unit/test_criteria_eval.py` — existing CLI tests (`test_cli_unknown_criterion_errors`, `test_cli_absent_fixture_errors`) exercise the same preflight; verify they remain green with the dual-registry lookup (unknown ids that match neither registry still exit 1).'
